coredata.dc:title: Fingerprint imaging using N-doped carbon dots
coredata.dc:creator: Milenkovic, Ivana
Algarra, Manuel
Alcoholado, Cristina
Cifuentes, Manuel
Lázaro-Martínez, Juan M.
Rodríguez-Castellón, Enrique
Mutavdžić, Dragosav
Radotić, Ksenija
Bandosz, Teresa J.
coredata.dc:description: Carbon Dots (CDs) were obtained using a hydrothermal method and used for the detection of fingerprints through fluorescent imaging. Synthesized CDs exhibited a brightness emission at 495 nm, which was related to their structural and chemical properties. The results of detailed surface characterizations by XPS, ss-NMR and fluorescence spectroscopies, suggested that the negative charge of the functionals groups promoted electrostatic interactions between the charged CDs surface functional groups (amine, amide and carboxylic) and the secretion components present in the thin layer of fluid left on the surface upon its direct contact with human fingers. The obtained results were validated by the scientific protocol of the Police Automated Fingerprint Identification System (AFIS) based on a biometric identification.

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: Authors thank to the projects CTQ2015-68951-C3-3-R ( MINECO , Spain), ANPCyT ( PICT 2012-0151 ), Univ. Buenos Aires ( UBACyT 2013–2016/043BA ) and CONICET ( PIP 2014-2016/130 ), and grant Nº 173017 ( Ministry of Education, Science and Technology of the Republic of Serbia ). The support from COST ( ACTION 16101 ) is acknowledged. We also thank SCAI (Málaga, Spain) for performing the NMR measurements. MA thanks ARDITI through the project M1420-01-0145-FEDER-000005-CQM + .

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM images of the N-CDs. Inset HRTEM and size histogram distribution. Reprinted/Adapted with permission from Ref. [26]. Copyright 2018, Elsevier. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
Deconvoluted C 1s, O 1s and N 1s core energy level spectra. Reprinted/Adapted with permission from Ref. [26]. Copyright 2018, Elsevier. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
UV Absorbance and emission spectra of N-CDs. Adapted with permission from Ref. [26]. Copyright 2018, Elsevier. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
15N CP-MAS (@15 kHz) (A), 13C CP-MAS (@15 kHz) (B) and 1H MAS NMR (@30 kHz) (C) spectra for N-CDs. Water signals are indicated with the letter W. The spinning-side bands are marked with an asterisk.

(A) Emission spectra of aqueous N-CDs under different excitation wavelengths and (B) Estimated emission contributions of N-CDs nanoparticles at 411 (green), 450 (pink) and 495 (blue); Emission spectra (black) and estimated emission spectra (red) of N-CDs under excitation 350 nm (excitation spectra: the inset of Figure B). (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
Comparison between the OD values of control and different concentrations of N-CDs treated cells (*P < 0.05). (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
Latent fingerprints images based on N-CDs nanoparticles on tested surfaces of (A) a metal tweezers and (B) a regular plastic PET bag.

(A) AFIS analysis for fingerprints images developed using N-CDs nanoparticles on a tweezers surface and validated by the corresponding matches obtained from a database using a traditional approach (B). (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

head.ce:title: Fingerprint imaging using N-doped carbon dots

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute for Multidisciplinary Research, University of Belgrade, Kneza Višeslava 1, 11000, Beograd, Serbia

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Organic Chemistry and IQUIMEFA-CONICET, Faculty of Pharmacy and Biochemistry, University of Buenos Aires, C1113AAD, Ciudad Autónoma de Buenos Aires, Argentina

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Organic Chemistry and IQUIMEFA-CONICET

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. CQM-Madeira Chemistry Research Centre, University of Madeira, Campus de Penteada, 9020-105, Funchal, Madeira, Portugal.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon Dots (CDs) were obtained using a hydrothermal method and used for the detection of fingerprints through fluorescent imaging. Synthesized CDs exhibited a brightness emission at 495 nm, which was related to their structural and chemical properties. The results of detailed surface characterizations by XPS, ss-NMR and fluorescence spectroscopies, suggested that the negative charge of the functionals groups promoted electrostatic interactions between the charged CDs surface functional groups (amine, amide and carboxylic) and the secretion components present in the thin layer of fluid left on the surface upon its direct contact with human fingers. The obtained results were validated by the scientific protocol of the Police Automated Fingerprint Identification System (AFIS) based on a biometric identification.

ce:section.ce:para: Fingerprints analysis has been used as a powerful and reliable tool for an individual identification in a forensic science for more than a century [1]. When a finger touches a solid surface, an imprint of the finger's ridge patterns arises. It is named a latent fingerprint and it is invisible to a naked eye. The traditional techniques, which lead to the identification of the latent fingerprints are those based on a powder dusting. To reveal images, they employ lead carbonate/sulfate [2], ferric oxide [3], ninhydrin spraying [4] and fluorescent dye staining [5]. Because in some of these techniques toxic agents are used, there is a need to find fluorescent biocompatible materials which can be employed for a fingerprint identification.

Carbon dots nanoparticles (CDs) might be promising candidates for this kind of applications [6] owing to the bright emission that they can produce upon irradiation. They have recently, gained the attention of scientists because of hydrophilicity, stable luminescence, photo-bleaching resistance, chemical inertness, low cost and easiness of their functionalization [7]. Their applications have been tested in various fields such as in printing inks [8,9], drug delivery [10,11], imaging [12–16] and chemical sensing [17–20], photocatalysts [21–23], optoelectronics [24,25]. A very important asset of these materials is their negligible toxicity [26] compared to conventional quantum dots [27–29]. Nevertheless, there are some features of CDs, which limit their application in forensic science. Examples are a self-fluorescence emission from the substrate, which creates an analytical interference or an aggregation effect, which quenched the emission process [30]. Therefore, there is a need to develop new synthesis approaches leading to CDs free of the above-mentioned limitations. The promising approaches might be those based on an incorporation of heteroatoms, such as nitrogen, to the CDs surface. Nitrogen species in the carbon matrix might provide additional electrons and result in an increase in the emission brightness. It has been reported that carbogenically coated silica nanoparticles or CDs coated in several steps with ethanolamine providing N atoms, improve the effect of fingerprint image visualization [31–34]. N-carbon sources such p-phenylenediamine [35], dimethylacrylamide [36], diammonium hydrogen citrate and urea [37], polydopamine film-coated poly(dimethylsiloxane) flake with dense plasmonic silver nanoparticles [38], glycine [39], Tween 80 [40] or malic acid and ammonium oxalate, obtained by a simple microwave approach [41], have been described as providing images for fingerprints detection.

The objective of this paper is the analysis of the application of N-doped CDs (N CDs) obtained from polyvinylpyrrolidone (PVP) for fingerprint imaging. The stress is on the effect of their surface features on the imaging performance. Optical spectroscopy and nuclear magnetic resonance methods are used to characterize CDs. They bring information about the bulk and surface features important for the fingerprint identification application. As an example of the applications, common daily-life surfaces such as a simple tweezers and a plastic bag were chosen to demonstrate fingerprint images provided by CDs. The novelty of our work is in applying N-containing carbon nanopowder obtained in a simple step process, without an introduction of an additional nitrogen source, for the efficient fingerprint detection. Moreover, our material can be used as-received and it is non-toxic, as opposite to a lead carbonate powder routinely used by Spanish police.

The N-CDs was synthesized using the hydrothermal treatment of polyvinylpyrrolidone (PVP), as N source. The details are described elsewhere [26]. PVP (1.8 g) was first dissolved in 50 mL of H2O, and added to a teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave, which was heated to 200 °C for 6 h. The obtained mixture was first filtered using a cylindrical filtration membrane filter (0.22 μm), and then washed with dichloromethane not miscible in water, easy removed under vacuum, inert to the chemistry of CDs). The solvent was removed by vacuum drying.

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurements were carried out on a Physical Electronics PHI VersaProbe II spectrometer using monochromatic Al-Kα radiation (49.1 W, 15 kV and 1486.6 eV). C 1s, O 1s and N 1s core-level spectra were analyzed. They were recorded with a constant pass energy value of 29.35 eV, using a 100 μm diameter circular analysis area, and analyzed using PHI SmartSoft software and processed using MultiPak 9.3 package. Shirley-type background and Gauss-Lorentz curves were used to determine the binding energies. The binding energy values were referenced to adventitious carbon C 1s signal (284.8 eV). High resolution NMR spectra were recorded using the ramp 1H 13C or 1H 15N CP-MAS sequence (cross-polarization and magic angle spinning) with proton decoupling during acquisition, respectively. The 13C/15N CP-MAS experiments were performed at a room temperature in a Bruker Avance-IIIHD 600 spectrometer equipped with a 3.2 mm MAS probe. The operating frequency for protons, carbon and nitrogen was 600.09, 150.91 and 60.80 MHz, respectively. Ammonium chloride was used as an external reference for the 15N spectra and glycine was used as an external reference for the 13C spectra and to set the Hartmann-Hahn matching condition in the cross-polarization experiments in both 15N and 13C spectra. The contact time during CP was 2000 and 3000 μs for 13C and 15N spectra, respectively. The SPINAL64 sequence (small phase incremental alternation with 64 steps) was used for heteronuclear decoupling during the acquisition [42]. The spinning rate for all samples was 15 kHz. The 1H-MAS experiments were performed in a 2.5-mm MAS probe with an operating frequency for protons of 600.09 MHz 1H chemical shifts were indirectly referenced relative to Si(CH3)4 using powdered glycine as an external reference. The spinning rate for all samples was 30 kHz. Steady state fluorescence spectra of N-CDs were collected using an FL3-221P spectrofluorometer (JobinYvon Horiba, Paris, France) equipped with a 450 W Xe lamp and a photomultiplier tube. The spectra of N CDs were measured in a front-face configuration of the measuring cavity. The slits on the excitation and emission beams were fixed both at 2 nm. The integration time was 0.1 s. The spectra were corrected for dark counts. The Rayleigh masking was applied to reduce Rayleigh scattering from the solid sample which limits the sensitivity and accuracy of the measurement. A series of 15 emission spectra were collected, by excitation at different wavelengths. The excitation and emission ranges were 350–392 and 405–680 nm, with 3 and 1 nm increment respectively.

Multivariate Curve Resolution-Alternate Least Squares (MCR-ALS) is a family of methods used to solve complex functions by determining the number of constituents, their response profiles (spectra, pH profiles, time profiles, elution profiles) and their estimated concentrations, when no prior information is available about the nature and composition of recorded signals [43]. Mathematically, MCR-ALS method can be expressed as bilinear model expressed by the following formula: D = CST + E, where C is the matrix whose columns describe excitation profiles of fluorophores. It consists of the number of rows that is equal to the number of excitation wavelengths (the number of emission spectra) and the number of columns that is equal to the number of detected fluorophores. ST is the matrix whose rows are the emission spectra and the number of columns corresponds to the number of emission wavelengths. E is matrix of residuals [44]. All analyses were performed using the Unscrambler software package (Camo ASA).

The standard 3-(4,5-dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-5-(3-carboxymethoxyphenyl)-2-(4-sulfophenyl)-2H-tetrazolium (MTS) assay was used to study the cytotoxicity effects of the N-CQDs. Canine adipose mesenchymal stem cells (cAd-MSCs) maintained in Dulbecco's Modified Eagle's Medium (D6546 Sigma Aldrich, Spain) supplemented with 10% (v/v) fetal bovine serum (F7524 Sigma Aldrich, Spain) at 37 °C in a humidified atmosphere of 5% carbon dioxide (CO2) was used for the assay. The cells were seeded at a density of 25 × 103 cells per 500 μL in 48-well plates and incubated for 24, 48 and 72 h with various concentrations of the doped carbon dots (0.01–0.0004 mg/mL) dissolved in the culture medium [45]. Then, 100 μL of MTS) (G3580, Promega®) was added to each well followed by 3 h of incubation in dark at 37 °C. Subsequently, 100 μL of each well was removed to a 96-well plate and the optical density (OD) was read on a multi-well microplate reader at 490 nm. Each condition was tested in quadruplicate, including control. Two-way ANOVA with post-Dunnett´s method was used to analyse data sets using Sigma Plot 11.0. For this analysis a P value (probability value) < 0.05 was considered statistically significant (marked on the plot as *). All experimental data are reported, and the mean and the error bars represent the experimental standard error (±standard deviation, SD).

Fingerprints were collected from one individual volunteer. After touching various selected surfaces, such as tweezers and a plastic bag of a local supermarket, the surfaces were covered with N-CQDs, to detect fingerprints. Such surfaces were chosen taking into account that the tweezers represent a polished metal surface and the plastic bag-a flexible surface of every day usage. The fingerprint images were obtained using a Multi-zoom Nikon AZ-100 microscope and a Nikon Digital Sight-5Mc, connected to the microscope by a computer, in which the pictures were stored and visualized by Nis-Elements software. The microscope was equipped with an epifluorescence system, in which the samples were illuminated by a mercury lamp (100 W) and a variety of available narrow band pass filter blocks (UV-2A: (EX 330–380 nm; DM 400 nm; BA 420 nm) were used.

After this, the recorded fingerprints were analyzed by 3 M Cogent CAFIS system (3 M Cogent's Automated Fingerprint and Palm Print Identification System), a customizable software package that allows to perform a wide range of tasks for processing, editing, searching, retrieving, and storing fingerprint images and subject records used by Scientific Police in Spain. In this way the positive identification of the individual by his/her fingerprints was achieved. Even though the fingerprints were collected from only one individual, we consider that their introduction to the police data system followed by a positive identification validate the applicability of our CDs for the fingerprints detection.
The morphology and chemistry of N-CDs has been studied by HR-TEM and was addressed in Ref. [26], Fig. 1 shows well dispersed spherical nanoparticles with a mean diameter of 6.5 nm.

The deconvoluted C 1s, O 1s and N 1s core energy level spectra are presented in Fig. 2 . The content of nitrogen and oxygen was 10.6 at. % and 13.2 at. %, respectively [26]. The deconvolution of the C 1s core energy level spectrum showed sp
2 graphitic carbon (majority), CO (phenolic, alcoholic, etheric), and carbon in carbonyl and carboxylic groups (at 284.8 eV, 286.0 eV, and 287.6 eV, respectively). A marked content of carbon in carbonyl groups was linked to amide groups on the surface N-CDs at 400.2 eV (100%). The O 1s core level spectrum revealed two contributions linked to alcohols and carbonyl groups.

UV absorption and emission spectra upon exciting at 350 nm of N-CDs is presented in Fig. 3 . The most intense emission was measured is at ∼450 nm. The quantum yield was 6% [26]. The broad emission is linked to both, heterogeneity of the dots' sizes and complex surface chemistry. It was found that different concentrations of PVP used in the synthesis led to the differences in the intensity of the emission spectrum (Fig. S1 of the Supplementary Information).

To collect information about the structural and chemical features of the N CDs material at the molecular level, 13C CP-MAS and 1H-MAS solid-state NMR spectra were recorded (Fig. 4A, B). The 13C resonance signals correspond to the chemical structure of poly(vinylpyrrolidone) used in the synthesis of the N-CDs nanoparticles. This indicates that the newly formed carbon nanostructures, are covered or that the nanostructures are dispersed within the polymeric matrix. The 15N CP-MAS NMR spectrum of the N-CDs sample shows a nitrogen resonance signal at −251.6 ppm corresponding to an amide nitrogen group of the poly(vinylpyrrolidone) (Fig. 4 A). The assignment of the NMR signals is provided in Table S1, of the Supplementary Information (SI) and it follows previous reports [46,47] based on ss-NMR experiments.

The results of 1H-MAS ss-NMR experiment are shown in Fig. 4C. In the 1H-MAS spectrum of N-CDs, the common 1H resonance signals (H5,6) that correspond to the ethylene segments of the polymeric structure are visible (Fig. 4C and Table S1 of the Supplementary Information). Other 1H resonance signals are well resolved and correspond to the pyrrolidin-2-one cyclic system (H2-4, Fig. 4C and Table S1 of the Supplementary Information [48]). Additionally, the 1H-MAS ss-NMR spectrum shows water signals around δ 1H of 4–7 ppm linked to strongly associated water molecules that are present on the surface of the samples. That water does not interact with the material and behaves as pure bulk water [47,49].

Another water signal at δ1H around 0–1 ppm is related to weakly associated water molecules which interact with the polymer/nanostructure surface through the hydrogen bonds or electrostatic interactions, especially with the participation of H3O+ (Fig. 4C) [49]. The bulk structural information related to the functionalization of N-CDs shows that the amide groups (CONH2) together with some water clusters may stabilize these systems. This might affect their luminescence properties that are discussed below [50]. The NMR analysis agrees with that of XPS discussed above [26].

Since the target application of the obtained N-CDs is the fingerprint imaging, the powder was characterized by steady state fluorescence spectroscopy. The emission spectrum of N-CDs (Fig. 5 A) is complex, with a large spectral width, apparently containing three emission maxima (Fig. 5). The excitation maximum is at 380 nm for the 450 nm emission (Fig. 5A). The MCR-ALS analysis showed that the emission spectrum of N-CDs contains three components with the maxima 411, 450 and 495 nm (Fig. 5B). They represent three different fluorophores corresponding to different structural/chemical patterns on the surface (in the front -phase configuration the emitted light comes from the surface) of the nanoparticles (from distinct surface functional groups), and demonstrate a surface heterogeneity of this compound. The fitted emission spectra (red line) (Fig. 5B) validate the three-component model used. This is in accordance with the 13C NMR results showing that in N-CDs the nanostructures are dispersed within the polymeric matrix. Also, the interactions of water with the amide groups leads to formation of clusters that stabilize the system (see the results of 1H-MAS, Fig. 4C) affecting the luminescence by contributing to the formation of various fluorophores/structural motifs. They arise due to π→π*, n→σ* and n→π* transitions associated with CC and carbonyl/hydroxyl groups, respectively [51]. The relative fluorescence yield, which is the ratio of the maximum intensities of the emission and corresponding excitation spectra, is 0.85.

The MTS assay was used to evaluate the cytotoxicity of N-CDs on mammalian cells. The OD value is directly proportional to the number of living cells, so higher OD values indicate a greater number of cells and less cytotoxicity of the product [52]. Results showed no cytotoxic effect of N-CDs when used in high concentrations (up to 0.01 μg/mL) for 72 h. However, statistically significant differences between the OD values of control and cells treated with the lowest concentration of N-CDs (0.0004 mg/mL) at 24, 48 and 72 h were observed (Fig. 6 ). These results suggest that CDs might aggregate when they are dissolved in the culture medium at high concentrations owing to dispersive interactions. We consider it as a plausible scenario although no experiments were performed to support this process. This would prevent their incorporation to the cells and in this way viability of the latter would not be affected. At a low concentration, the aggregation state likely does not occur and N-CDs might be incorporated to the cells affecting their viability. Therefore, these results demonstrate the biocompatibility of N-CDs at high concentrations ensuring the cell viability. At 0.01 mg/mL and higher concentrations they can be considered as innocuous and not causing any toxic effect.

N-CDs were used as fluorescence labels to image fingerprints on two types of surfaces: a steel tweezers (Fig. 7 A) and on the surface of a plastic (PET) bag (Fig. 7B). Both surfaces revealed the acceptable fingerprint images, displaying well resolved ridges, bifurcations and lakes in a pale orange emission, when irradiated at 360 nm. This emission presented in Fig. 7 is obtained using filters barriers, that transmit the sufficiently long wavelength to enable a photographic imaging, minimizing the fluorescence background. The resulting image should be photographed using a filter that transmits the same wavelengths as the filter used for viewing [53], therefore the fingerprint details could be clearly recognized.

The ζ potential of this material was reported to be −6.47 mV and the slightly negative charge is associated to the abundance of surface OH and COOH groups on N-CDs [36]. The mechanism for imaging fingerprints could be explained by the electrostatic interactions of the charged CDs surface functional groups (amine, amide and carboxylic) with basic proteins of the secretion components, as previously reported [34].

The automated fingerprint identification (AFIS) was used to evaluate the applicability of CQDs as detection systems. In this process a computer matches fingerprints against a large database of known and unknown fingerprints. The results found are shown in Fig. 8 . The numbers of features match required by law varies, depending on the country. In Spain minimum 12 matches are needed. As seen, on the surface of the tweezers 12 matches were easy identified. The results are also compared to those found by the AFIS system using one of the usual protocols in Scientific Police of Spain. The experimental results show the satisfactory performance of N-CDs when used for fingerprint detection. The carbon nanomaterial used is fluorescent and non-toxic, and the images obtained are brighter than those obtained with an ink.

N doped carbon dots (N-CDs) synthesized from PVP polymer by a hydrothermal approach render a bright fluorescence signal when excited at 350 nm. When used as a fingerprint detection medium, the bright patterns of unique features were detected. The results of chemical analyses suggested that surface functional groups of N-CDs, mainly OCNH, were responsible for interactions with biomolecules and those interaction led to specific fluorescence patterns. Three main components were identified in the fluorescence emission, originating from different fluorophores/structural patterns on the surface of N-CDs nanoparticles. N-CDs were found as nontoxic when used in concentration over 0.01 mg/mL and this feature, along with the fingerprint imaging quality suggest that those carbonaceous nanomaterials can be successfully used in forensic science.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Authors thank to the projects CTQ2015-68951-C3-3-R (MINECO, Spain), ANPCyT (PICT 2012-0151), Univ. Buenos Aires (UBACyT 2013–2016/043BA) and CONICET (PIP 2014-2016/130), and grant Nº 173017 (Ministry of Education, Science and Technology of the Republic of Serbia). The support from COST (ACTION 16101) is acknowledged. We also thank SCAI (Málaga, Spain) for performing the NMR measurements. MA thanks ARDITI through the project M1420-01-0145-FEDER-000005-CQM+.

Carbon dots based nanopowders and their application for fingerprint recovery
Fast one-step synthesis of N-doped carbon dots by pyrolyzing ethanolamine

An improved broadband decoupling sequence for liquid crystals and solids

A Study of blending and complexation of poly(acrylic acid)/poly(vinyl pyrrolidone)
